Module Overloading
Bypassing EDR/XDR via Trust Inheritance

SN
Simon Ngoy · Security Researcher · Red Team / Windows Internals

Introduction

Security teams have never been better equipped. Modern EDR and XDR solutions incorporate kernel callbacks, real-time behavioral telemetry, machine learning, and near-total visibility into system events. On paper, an attacker has nowhere left to hide.

And yet.

The Evolution of EDR/XDR Solutions

Ten years ago, an antivirus compared file signatures. Bypassing this protection was as simple as modifying a few bytes of the payload to invalidate the signature. The race was straightforward, almost mechanical.

Today, the reality is radically different. A modern EDR simultaneously monitors:

  • User layerHooks in ntdll.dlleach syscall intercepted and analyzed in real time before reaching the kernel.
  • Kernel layerKernel callbacks for process, thread, and handle creation. ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) for deep telemetry.
  • Memory layerContinuous scan of allocated regions, their types (MEM_PRIVATE, MEM_IMAGE) and permissions.
  • Network layerTelemetry of all outgoing connections, protocol analysis, behavioral profiling.
  • File layerMinifilter driver intercepting each disk access - read, write, or execution.

Classic techniques that worked in 2018, VirtualAlloc with RWX permissions, CreateRemoteThread, injection via WriteProcessMemory are today detected in milliseconds. They have become behavioral signatures just as recognizable as static signatures.

Why Classic Techniques Are No Longer Sufficient

The fundamental problem with traditional approaches is that they are noisy by nature. They leave traces across every layer monitored by the EDR:

Classic injection : CreateRemoteThread + VirtualAlloc RWX 
Anonymous MEM_PRIVATE region .......... +1  memory type alert
RWX permissions ........................ +1  W+X combination alert
No associated file ..................... +1  anonymous region alert
CreateRemoteThread ..................... +1  cross-process thread alert
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Result: 4+ simultaneous indicators  -  IMMEDIATE ALERT  X

Each individual indicator may seem minor. Combined, they instantly exceed the detection threshold of any modern XDR. Detection is no longer a question of if, it is a question of how many milliseconds.

Faced with this reality, the question is no longer "how to hide the payload" but "how to never look like a threat".

What if Windows' Own Protection Became Its Attack Vector?

Windows places implicit trust in its own components. Microsoft system DLLs are signed, referenced, and well-known, and this trust is inherited by the security solutions running on top of it. A Microsoft module is not scanned with the same suspicion as an unknown binary. This is a deliberate trade-off, necessary to avoid thousands of daily false positives.

But this trust is transferable.

Module Overloading exploits precisely this mechanism: by injecting an arbitrary payload into the memory space of a legitimate Microsoft DLL, one inherits its reputation, its MEM_IMAGE memory type, its association with a known file on disk, and the blind trust the security solution grants it.

Result
A complete execution of Mimikatz, including credential extraction and a full C2 agent, without triggering a single alert against Sophos XDR and Windows Defender. Windows' own protection became its own attack vector.

Technical Background

2.1 PE Format and Sections

Every executable file under Windows, whether a .exe or a .dll - follows the PE (Portable Executable) format. This format is the foundation upon which everything explained in this article rests. Understanding it means understanding why Module Overloading works.

A PE file is structured like a building: a common entrance (the headers) that describes the structure, and independent floors (the sections) that each have their own purpose and access rules.

Each section in the Section Table is described by a set of attributes, including the memory permissions that will be applied when the file is loaded:

Section Permissions Reason
.text RX Code executes, but it never writes itself.
.data RW Data can be read and written, but not executed.
.rdata R One constant: reading only, nothing else.
.reloc R Relocation table: consulted on load, never executed.
W^X Principle
This strict separation of permissions is a fundamental security principle inherited from W^X (Write XOR Execute): a memory region should never be both writable and executable. This principle will be central to our analysis.

When Windows loads a PE into memory, it doesn't simply perform a byte-by-byte copy. It applies a memory alignment different from the file alignment, resolves imports, applies relocations, and assigns each section its permissions as defined in the Section Table. This loading process produces what is called a MEM_IMAGE type memory region.

2.2 MEM_PRIVATE vs MEM_IMAGE

Windows categorizes a process's memory regions into several types. The two that directly concern us are MEM_PRIVATE and MEM_IMAGE, and the difference between the two is central to the entire logic of Module Overloading evasion.

Type Origin Identity EDR Perspective
MEM_PRIVATE VirtualAlloc None : anonymous Wasteland : suspicious by default
MEM_IMAGE Windows mapping mechanism Associated PE file on disk Registered owner : trusted

In a healthy process, MEM_PRIVATE regions correspond to normal allocations: heap, stack, buffers. MEM_IMAGE regions correspond to loaded modules. A MEM_PRIVATE region with execute permissions but no associated module is a glaring anomaly. This is precisely why classic VirtualAlloc-based injections are so easily detected.

2.3 Why RWX Is an Immediate Red Flag

PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE (RWX) protection is probably the most closely monitored indicator by modern EDRs. It combines write and execute permissions in the same region, which has no justification in legitimate code. When an EDR detects a MEM_PRIVATE region with RWX permissions, it sees exactly the pattern of a classic shellcode injection:

The natural workaround is to separate the write and execute phases: first allocate in RW, write the payload, then switch back to RX. This is better, but VirtualProtect on a region transitioning from RW to RX is itself a monitored signal. The real solution is to avoid MEM_PRIVATE altogether and that's precisely what Module Overloading does.

Approach Memory Type Permission Suspicion Level
VirtualAlloc RWX MEM_PRIVATE RWX MAXIMUM
VirtualAlloc RX MEM_PRIVATE RX HIGH
SEC_IMAGE .text MEM_IMAGE RX Weak

2.4 Introduction to Windows Mapping

Mapping a file into memory means making its contents directly accessible within a process's address space via a kernel object called a Section, without any manual byte copying. This is the technical foundation upon which all of Module Overloading's mechanics rest.

disk FILE
xpsservices.dll
→
kernel object SECTION
hSection
→
process memory VIEW
0x00007FF800000000

The SEC_IMAGE flag is particularly important: it instructs Windows to parse the PE, align sections according to their Virtual Addresses, apply the correct permissions, and register the resulting region as MEM_IMAGE, associated with a known file on disk. This is the trust that Module Overloading will hijack.

Module Overloading: Theory

3.1 Comparison with Module Stomping

To understand Module Overloading, one must first understand its direct predecessor: Module Stomping. Both techniques share the same fundamental intuition, exploiting a legitimate MEM_IMAGE region as an injection vector but diverge in their approach and scope.

Module Stomping is a surgical technique. It maps a sacrificial DLL into memory, then partially overwrites its .text section with a position-independent shellcode. The rest of the DLL remains intact. This approach has an intrinsic limitation: a position-independent shellcode by definition lacks PE structure, sufficient for a simple stager, but insufficient for executing a full PE like Mimikatz.

Module Overloading removes this constraint. Instead of a shellcode, the payload is a complete PE, an entire DLL or EXE. And instead of partially overwriting the .text section, it replaces the entirety of the sacrificial DLL, starting with its own PE headers.

Key Insight
The MEM_IMAGE region and its association to the file on disk do not change; it is the identity of the mapping, not its content, that defines this type of region. The content has been entirely replaced, but the EDR continues to see a MEM_IMAGE region associated with a known DLL.

Module Stomping also carries an additional weakness: the NtCreateThreadEx call used to start execution is itself a heavily monitored kernel event. PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine fires immediately, and the EDR inspects the thread's StartAddress. When that address points into the middle of a .text section at an unusual offset mismatching the file on disk, detection becomes straightforward. Module Overloading combined with Thread Hijacking eliminates this signal entirely: no new thread is ever created.

3.2 The Sacrificial DLL Concept

The sacrificial DLL is the pivot of the entire technique. It provides the identity that the payload will inherit, its name, its reputation, its association with a signed Microsoft file on disk. Its selection is not trivial: a poor choice can transform a discreet technique into an immediate alert.

  • Signed by MicrosoftThe most important criterion, grants maximum trust in the Windows ecosystem.
  • Rarely loaded by legitimate processesA second mapping of an already-loaded DLL may draw attention from behavioral heuristics.
  • Large enough to accommodate the payloadSizeOfImage must exceed the total payload size including all sections.
  • Few dependencies and no sensitive sectionsNeutral and unremarkable - the ideal ghost in the process memory map.

3.3 Why SEC_IMAGE Is Key

SEC_IMAGE is the flag that transforms a simple file mapping into a memory region with a module identity. Without it, NtCreateSection produces a raw mapping the resulting region is MEM_MAPPED, functional but without identity. The EDR sees it as an anonymous region. With SEC_IMAGE, Windows triggers a full load process:

The result is a region that Windows itself recognizes as a loaded module. This recognition propagates throughout the security chain:

Trust propagation chain 
Windows : "This MEM_IMAGE region comes from xpsservices.dll"
EDR     : "xpsservices.dll = signed Microsoft DLL = TRUST"
Result  :  No alert triggered, execution proceeds undetected
Important Nuance
The association between the MEM_IMAGE region and the source file is established at the time of mapping. It doesn't depend on the region's content. Even after completely overwriting the sacrificial DLL with our payload, the region remains identified as MEM_IMAGE from xpsservices.dll. The identity resides in the mapping metadata, not in the bytes.

3.4 The Concept of Trust Inheritance

Trust Inheritance is the implicit mechanism that explains why Module Overloading works against advanced security solutions. It's not a flaw in Windows code. It's an exploitation of a design assumption in security systems. This assumption can be stated simply:

"Microsoft modules are safe. Anything that runs in their context can be trusted."

This assumption is reasonable. It allows security solutions to function without raising alarms about the thousands of legitimate operations performed daily by system modules. Without it, every call to ntdll.dll or kernel32.dll would have to be scrutinized with the same suspicion as an unknown binary, rendering any security solution unusable in production.

Module Overloading exploits this design assumption: by inhabiting a trusted module's memory space, the payload inherits its trust unconditionally.

3.5 Module Overloading's Achilles' Heel

Module Overloading is not theoretically undetectable. The disk/memory content mismatch is its fundamental weakness and tools like Pe-sieve, Moneta, or Patriot scan for it explicitly:

Mismatch detection algorithm pe-sieve / Moneta 
On disk:
xpsservices.dll  [MZ][PE][.text original][.data]   hash = AAA

In memory after overloading:
MEM_IMAGE region [MZ][PE][.text = PAYLOAD][.data]  hash = BBB  : DIFFERENT!

Scanner algorithm:
for each MEM_IMAGE region in process:
  source_file = get_mapped_file(region)
  disk_bytes  = read_file(source_file)
  if compare_sections(disk_bytes, region) != MATCH:
    ALERT: disk/memory mismatch detected

Disk/memory mismatch is detectable in theory, but in practice, EDRs make performance compromises that create blind spots. Running a full I/O-intensive mismatch scan on every MEM_IMAGE region at all times is computationally prohibitive. It is precisely in these blind spots that techniques like Module Overloading thrive.

OPSEC Offensive: The Philosophy of Minimal Noise

The Logic of the Trigger Threshold

Understanding why Module Overloading works in real-world conditions requires understanding how an EDR makes its decisions. A modern EDR doesn't operate in a binary fashion, it continuously calculates a suspicion score based on the accumulation of observed indicators:

ActionIndicators Generated
VirtualAlloc RWX
+5
OpenProcess(LSASS)
+6
CreateRemoteThread
+4
SeDebugPrivilege
+3
Write to suspicious file path
+4
EDR trigger threshold
22 pts
Attacker score comparison 
Noisy attacker  = 22 points  ->  IMMEDIATE ALERT  X
Silent attacker =  8 points  ->  BELOW THRESHOLD  Ok

This threshold is an operational necessity for the security vendor. Setting it too low generates thousands of false positives, rendering the solution unusable in production. Setting it too high allows real attacks to slip through. It's a constant and exploitable, trade-off.

Staying Below the Threshold

The effectiveness of Module Overloading against EDR and Windows Defender isn't based on a magic bullet. It relies on strict discipline: minimizing the array of indicators to prevent the I/O-intensive mismatch scan from ever being triggered.

The result is straightforward: no triggering events occur. Without a triggering event, the I/O-intensive mismatch scan is never initiated. Without a mismatch scan, the different content in memory is never compared to the file on disk. The technique remains invisible not because it's theoretically undetectable, but because it never gives the EDR a reason to search.

The golden rule: An EDR doesn't detect what it isn't looking for. It only looks for what has been triggered. Therefore: never trigger.
Implementation Note : Minimal Footprint
Many public loaders routinely include AMSI patching, ETW bypasses, and other workarounds just in case, without evaluating whether these techniques are relevant to their execution vector. Each unnecessary patch generates additional indicators without providing any benefit - a fundamental opsec error. In this implementation: XOR encryption of the payload + Module Overloading only. Nothing more. Two techniques. Zero unnecessary noise.

Results: Bypassing Sophos XDR & Windows Defender

Two tests were conducted on live, updated systems with active security solutions rated among the best by Gartner to validate the technique end-to-end.

Test 1 : Mimikatz with Full Credential Extraction

Complete execution of mimikatz.exe x64, including sekurlsa::logonpasswords credential extraction, running via the Module Overloading loader. The Sophos XDR dashboard confirms simultaneously: "No malware or PUAs" and "Sophos software: No issues found", while the Mimikatz banner and mimikatz # prompt are fully visible in the terminal.

Test 2 : Full C2 Agent via AdaptixC2

A second test using AdaptixC2 as the C2 framework, deploying a full implant agent via the Module Overloading loader. The agent successfully established a beacon, listing active sessions with PID, architecture, and session metadata in the framework UI. Sophos XDR: no detections.

Conclusion

Module Overloading illustrates a fundamental trend in modern offensive security: the most effective techniques are not those that try to hide, but those that exploit the design assumptions of defenses.

By inheriting the trust that Windows and XDR solutions grant to Microsoft DLLs, Module Overloading manages to execute an arbitrary payload, including Mimikatz with credential extraction, and a full C2 agent via AdaptixC2 without triggering a single alert on Sophos XDR or Windows Defender.

Fundamental Limitation Exposed
If an attacker can impersonate a trusted entity, doors open. Every reputation-based approach carries this structural weakness. The only durable answer is architectural isolation, Credential Guard, HVCI, isolated execution zones, that makes access physically impossible regardless of the caller's identity.
No security system is entirely watertight. Understanding how it fails is the first step toward building something that doesn't.
Simon Ngoy : Security Researcher / Red Team / Windows Internals
